3: <header>1. Introduction: industrial symbiosis to reduce the environmental impacts of agriculture </header>

Agriculture accounts for 14% of global anthropogenic emissions of greenhouse gases (GHGs) (IPCC, 2014). The main sources of agricultural GHG emissions are N2O from nutrient management and CH4 from ani- mal digestive processes and manure (Paustian et al., 2006), both asso- ciated with animal husbandry. Improved farm management practices and bioenergy production from waste could enable agriculture not only to reduce these GHG emissions (Smith et al., 2007) but to offset some of the emissions from other sectors (Paustian et al., 2006). Agriculture is also responsible for a range of local environmental impacts. Failure to manage nitrogen and phosphorus is a serious issue recognised globally: anthropogenic activities may have already pushed nutrient cycles out of the "safe space for humanity" (Rockström et al., 2009). Within the agricultural sector, management of manure from animal husbandry is a specific but widespread problem. Conventional storage and spreading of manure emit nearly 200 compounds (O'Neill and Phillips, 1992),

causing environmental and public health concerns. During conventional surface spreading of manure, 25-66% of the total available nitrogen can be lost via ammonia volatilization (Manitoba Agriculture, Food and Rural Initiatives, 2009). Surface spreading can also contaminate ground water and surface water, causing eutrophication through both nitrogen and phosphorus in the run-off (Clift and Shaw, 2012). Serious water pollution has been reported in many areas with high livestock density, including dairy farming in southwest Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and pig farming in Brittany, France (Chrisafis, 2019) and North Carolina, USA (Hellerstein and Fine, 2020).

Mitigating the impacts of agricultural production will require sig- nificant changes in agricultural practices. Anaerobic Digestion (AD) is a technology to convert organic matter to biogas, containing typically 60% CH4 and 40% CO2, which can be used to substitute natural gas. A number of studies have found that AD can avoid manure storage (Chowdhury et al., 2018), reduce direct CH4 emissions (Clemens et al., 2006; Weiske et al., 2006), and produce methane for use as a source of energy (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006). Digestion converts organic

nitrogen into ammonium, which is retained in the slurry residue called digestate. Risberg et al. (2017) showed that the ammonium content in digestate (1.9-5.3 kg/ton) is higher than that in undigested dairy manure (0.7-3.2 kg/ton) Applying digestate close to its source, as a fertiliser (Stoknes et al., 2016) or soil conditioner, displaces synthetic fertilisers (Lukehurst et al., 2010). However, implementation of AD and use of digestate in dairy farming can cause profound changes in nitrogen cycling by concentrating nitrogen locally, as reported in Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and the Netherlands (Hoang et al., 2020). The high cost of transporting surplus digestate away from the AD facility increases the risk of improper spreading and consequent nitrogen loss (British Columbia, 2011). In some rare cases, surplus digestate can be concen- trated using membrane technology and sold (Shandong Minhe Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., 2020); however, due to costs and environ- mental impacts arising from the additional separation process, this op- tion is only viable with both supportive policy and favorable fertilizer prices.

The approach of Industrial Symbiosis (IS) creates symbiotic networks of participants by exchange of materials and energy, often in the form of by-products (Chertow and Park, 2016). In the agricultural sector, IS frequently centres on AD of animal manure. In a typical AD-centred integrated system, biogas is combusted to provide energy to the ani- mal farm and to some neighbouring farms. Digestate is utilized in an extended network of agricultural activities to reduce inorganic fertiliser use and nutrient surplus (Stoknes et al., 2016). Fodder and residues of food crops produced within the integrated system can be fed to animals, leading to further closed-loop flows. A number of AD-centred integrated systems have been reported in China, specifically in Laiwu (Qi et al., 2005), Lengzibao and Xiyingzi (Cheng et al., 2011), and Yantai (Shan- dong Minhe Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., 2020), with various combinations of digester, animal production, and greenhouse. Alfaro and Miller (2013) described an integrated system incorporating husbandry of pigs, rabbits, and fish with a rice mill in Liberia. A "food to waste to food" system in Norway integrates anaerobic digestion, vegetable cultivation, and mushroom growing (Stoknes et al., 2016).

However, despite analyses of environmental benefits of stand-alone AD units or case studies introducing AD-centred integrated systems, studies that quantitatively investigate the overall environmental im- pacts of AD-centred integrated systems are limited. In previous work, the environmental benefits of integrating dairy and greenhouse production in the Southern area of the Canadian province of British Columbia (BC) have been confirmed (Zhang et al., 2013). Further adding mushroom farming into an AD-centred integrated system can generate extra reve- nues and reduce GHG mitigation costs effectively (Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, building on previous findings, the present work aims to (1) explore the feasibility of building such an extended Eco-Industrial Park (EIP) linking dairy, greenhouse, and mushroom farming and (2) inves- tigate the farm-level and regional environmental consequences of such an EIP using life cycle assessment (LCA) and nutrient flow analysis, based on a case study in BC. Section 2 of this paper reviews the agri- culture sector in British Columbia, identifies activities that could be linked, and sets out the quantitative parameters for a model EIP. Section 3 sets out the methodologies life cycle assessment and nutrient flow analysis - used to investigate the environmental consequences of the model EIP. Section 4 presents the environmental benefits of the EIP and explores the feasibility of introducing this approach to an area with intensive agriculture. The overall conclusions are summarised in Section 5. Though drawn from a localised case study, the conclusions should extend to other areas with similar climates and intensive agricultural activities.

The approach of Industrial Symbiosis (IS) creates symbiotic networks of participants by exchange of materials and energy, often in the form of by-products (Chertow and Park, 2016). In the agricultural sector, IS frequently centres on AD of animal manure. In a typical AD-centred integrated system, biogas is combusted to provide energy to the ani- mal farm and to some neighbouring farms. Digestate is utilized in an extended network of agricultural activities to reduce inorganic fertiliser use and nutrient surplus (Stoknes et al., 2016). Fodder and residues of food crops produced within the integrated system can be fed to animals, leading to further closed-loop flows. A number of AD-centred integrated systems have been reported in China, specifically in Laiwu (Qi et al., 2005), Lengzibao and Xiyingzi (Cheng et al., 2011), and Yantai (Shan- dong Minhe Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., 2020), with various combinations of digester, animal production, and greenhouse. Alfaro and Miller (2013) described an integrated system incorporating husbandry of pigs, rabbits, and fish with a rice mill in Liberia. A "food to waste to food" system in Norway integrates anaerobic digestion, vegetable cultivation, and mushroom growing (Stoknes et al., 2016).

However, despite analyses of environmental benefits of stand-alone AD units or case studies introducing AD-centred integrated systems, studies that quantitatively investigate the overall environmental im- pacts of AD-centred integrated systems are limited. In previous work, the environmental benefits of integrating dairy and greenhouse production in the Southern area of the Canadian province of British Columbia (BC) have been confirmed (Zhang et al., 2013). Further adding mushroom farming into an AD-centred integrated system can generate extra reve- nues and reduce GHG mitigation costs effectively (Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, building on previous findings, the present work aims to (1) explore the feasibility of building such an extended Eco-Industrial Park (EIP) linking dairy, greenhouse, and mushroom farming and (2) inves- tigate the farm-level and regional environmental consequences of such an EIP using life cycle assessment (LCA) and nutrient flow analysis, based on a case study in BC. Section 2 of this paper reviews the agri- culture sector in British Columbia, identifies activities that could be linked, and sets out the quantitative parameters for a model EIP. Section 3 sets out the methodologies life cycle assessment and nutrient flow analysis - used to investigate the environmental consequences of the model EIP. Section 4 presents the environmental benefits of the EIP and explores the feasibility of introducing this approach to an area with intensive agriculture. The overall conclusions are summarised in Section 5. Though drawn from a localised case study, the conclusions should extend to other areas with similar climates and intensive agricultural activities.
8: <header>2. Case study: industrial symbiosis in agriculture in British Columbia </header>

British Columbia (BC), the westernmost province of Canada, has a substantial agricultural sector. Agriculture in BC resembles intensive agriculture in many other parts of the world and therefore can provide an informative case study and general insights. Cattle farming, mainly for dairy products, is one of the major agricultural activities, accounting for 26.8% of total farm receipts in the province (BC Ministry of Agri- culture, 2018). BC has 154 vegetable greenhouses (Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada, 2019) which accounts for nearly 10% of the prov- ince's total farm sales and more than 20% of total Canadian greenhouse vegetable sales (BC Ministry of Agriculture, 2018). These activities are heavily concentrated in the south of the province, in the region known as the Lower Mainland containing urban Metro Vancouver and the sub- urban and peri-urban area of the Fraser Valley.

Agriculture in the Fraser Valley contributes 16% of cattle and 67% of poultry production in the province but only has 2.5% of the agricultural land to provide a nutrient sink (British Columbia, 2017). The same problem can be observed in the Metro Vancouver region, with 3% of cattle and 23% of poultry production in the province but only 1.3% of agricultural land. Of the farms in BC, 62% had no formal assessment of their environmental impacts in 2017 (Statistics Canada, 2019a). The extent of improper manure management is unknown, but it will inevi- tably increase nutrient losses. Furthermore, 49% of BC's population lives in Metro Vancouver (Statistics Canada, 2019b), generating food waste that calls for composting and land application. These statistics underline why nutrient management is a problem in regions like the Lower Mainland of BC.

Greenhouse cultivation accounts for more than 36% of the total natural gas consumption in BC's agriculture sector (Khakbazan, 2000), much of it for heating in the cool BC climate. In summer, heat demand is low but significant amounts of natural gas are still used to provide CO2-enriched greenhouse atmosphere to enhance plant growth. Zhang et al. (2013) explored the possibility of linking dairy farming with greenhouse production in BC. The biogas is used locally to replace natural gas and thereby avoid the need to purify the methane. The digestate is separated into a liquid phase used as fertilizer and a solid phase used as cow bedding and greenhouse growing medium. An envi- ronmental Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) confirmed that this form of symbiosis can achieve significant environmental and resource im- provements but showed that performance can be improved further if two issues are addressed: use of natural gas for CO2 enrichment in summer with the combustion heat wasted; and disposal of surplus digestate. The latter problem was also identified by Hoang et al. (2020).

We therefore look for alternative sources of CO2 and users of diges- tate. Mushroom growing, another activity in the Lower Mainland of BC, emits CO2 continuously from biological respiration, SO that ventilation is required to maintain air composition in mushroom farms. Furthermore, mushroom growing medium consists mainly of compost conditioned with balanced nutrients; horse and chicken manure are commonly im- ported to raise the nitrogen content, but the solid fraction of AD diges- tate could also be used. Thus the dairy farm-greenhouse system examined by Zhang et al. (2013) could potentially be expanded by introducing new players, i.e. mushroom farms, to enhance energy and material self-efficiency. Fig. 1 shows the principal flows linking the three elements of the resulting EIP.

The changes in non-renewable energy consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and human health impacts that would result from extending the EIP in this way have been explored by LCA, complemented by a substance flow analysis to explore the effects on regional nutrient flows. The quantitative parameters of the system modeled are set out in the next section.
Fig. 1. Energy and material flows in the extended Eco-Industrial Park.

13: The parameters describing the activities making up the model EIP are summarised in Table 1. The EIP centres on a hypothetical dairy farm with 500 cows. This represents a medium-sized farm in North America but is larger than the average size in BC (see Section 4.2) and is much larger than a typical European farm (Hoang et al., 2020). For the pur- poses of the modelling exercise, greenhouse and mushroom production in the EIP are sized to fit with the dairy farm by eliminating natural gas demand in the summer season: biogas combustion supplies the summer heat demand of the three farms while the mushroom farm provides the additional CO2 for the greenhouses. In colder seasons, additional heat- ing is provided by natural gas.
In conventional practice, the manure and water from washing down

the dairy farm are stored in a covered tank before being spread onto nearby crop land using a vacuum tank tractor. In the EIP, the manure is treated in a digester, modeled as a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) controlled at 35 °C for mesophilic digestion. The raw biogas is assumed to contain 60% CH4, 37.9% CO2, 2% H2O, and 0.1% H2S, which is a typical composition with a few trace impurities neglected, primarily the mercaptans that are a main cause of odour (BC Ministry of Envi- ronment, 2010). The raw biogas is passed through two fixed-bed adsorption columns filled respectively with limestone and iron filings to remove water vapour and H2S. Fugitive emissions are assumed to be 0.5% of the raw biogas from the digester (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories et al., 2010) and 0.2% of the upgraded biogas from the adsorption system (Börjesson and Berglund, 2006).
An electric press-screw separator is modeled to separate the digestate
Basic data for the farms in the EIP.

Item|Size|Size||||Unit Dairy farm|500|||||cattle Greenhouse farm|10,400|||||m² Mushroom farm land area|3,250|||||m² Mushroom farm bed area|9,750|||||m² |Monthly||||Annual Total| Biogas production|36.9||||442.8|1000 m³ Dried solid digestate generation|114||||1,368|tonne Liquid digestate generation|791||||9,492|tonne Sawdust bedding demand in dairy farm|42||||504|tonne Rockwool growing medium demand in greenhouse|1.1||||13.2|tonne Straw growing medium demand in mushroom farm|53||||636|tonne Nitrogen fertilizer demand in greenhouse|78||||936|kg N Surplus liquid digestate|1.1||||13.2|tonne N |Spring|Summer|Fall|Winter|Annual Total| Dairy farm heating demand|114|4|97|185|1,200|GJ Greenhouse heating demand|1,487|603|1,144|1,809|15,129|GJ Mushroom farm heating demand|254|10|215|410|2,667|GJ Greenhouse CO2 demand|108|144|108|14|1,122|tonne Natural gas consumption in base scenario|1,928|2,572|1,928|2,561|26,967|GJ Digester heating demand|134|93|127|158|1,536|GJ Heat supply from biogas|710|710|710|710|8,520|GJ Natural gas supplement for heating|1,279|0|872|1,851|12,006|GJ CO2 supply from flue gas|119|74|100|127|1,260|tonne Additional CO2 supply from mushroom farm|0|70|8|0|234|tonne

into a solid phase and a liquid phase. Solid digestate is assumed to be dried naturally with no energy consumption (Torenvliet, 2011). In winter when the climate is humid and cold in BC, additional drying is likely to be needed to prepare solid digestate for the use as animal bedding. Depending on the moisture content achievable by natural drying, the subsequent electrical drying may consume energy up to MJ/kg digestate (Rehl and Müller, 2011). However, compared to the rest of the EIP system, this energy consumption is insignificant and thus neglected. Part of the liquid digestate is spread on land using the tractor currently used for manure, and part of dried solid digestate is used in the dairy barn to substitute sawdust as animal bedding. The remaining digestate is exploited as value-added by-products to replace raw mate- rials used in greenhouse and mushroom farms (see below). Where sur- plus digestate needs to be composted (Scenario c; see Section 3.1), windrow composting is modeled.

The crop produced in the greenhouse in the EIP is modeled as pep- pers. Conventionally, the growing medium is a commercial rockwool product, and synthetic nitrogen fertiliser (urea or ammonium nitrate) is used as nitrogen fertilizer. In the EIP, dried solid digestate was taken to replace an equal mass of rockwool (Zhang et al., 2013) while liquid digestate substitutes synthetic nitrogen fertiliser at a rate that provides an equal nitrogen supply (Stoknes et al., 2016).

The mushroom farm is assumed to grow Pleurotus ostreatus, a crop for which it is feasible to substitute wheat straw in the growing medium by digestate (Isikhuemhen et al., 2009b). Mushrooms are typically grown in trays or on shelves on an indoor farm; the model is based on culti- vation with three levels of stacked trays. Ventilation rate varies throughout a mushroom growing cycle but, on average, 7.2 kg CO2 is released from 1 m² growing area per month to give an average con- centration of 815 ppm in mushroom houses (van Griensven, 1988). This is within the range 500-1,000 ppm typically used for stimulating plant growth in greenhouses (Luczynski and Portree, 2005). Therefore, the model assumes that ventilation air from the mushroom farm is piped untreated to the greenhouses. This air may contain pollutants or mushroom spores SO that, at very least, HEPA filtration would be necessary. In current operation, synthetic nitrogen fertiliser is added to the growing medium. The EIP avoids this input; instead, the growing medium contains 15% dried solid digestate, following studies showing that substrates containing 10-20% solid digestate, 70-80% wheat straw, and 10-20% millet gave the highest yield for two species of mushrooms (Isikhuemhen et al., 2009a, 2009b).

Heating demand for the farms is based on BC's climate, with seasonal variations considered to estimate the natural gas required to supplement the biogas. Natural gas or upgraded biogas is burned in a modulating boiler; the higher heating values are assumed to be 38 MJ/m³ (Fortis BC, 2012) and 22 MJ/m³ (Bartok, 2004), respectively. Electricity is from the grid and is represented as the average mix for BC in 2007: 93.35% hydro, 5.1% natural gas, 1.45% biomass and 0.1% liquid fossil fuel (Environ- ment Canada, 2011). Transportation is modeled as heavy-duty diesel-- powered vehicles.

Ventilation rate varies throughout a mushroom growing cycle but, on average, 7.2 kg CO2 is released from 1 m² growing area per month to give an average con- centration of 815 ppm in mushroom houses (van Griensven, 1988). This is within the range 500-1,000 ppm typically used for stimulating plant growth in greenhouses (Luczynski and Portree, 2005). Therefore, the model assumes that ventilation air from the mushroom farm is piped untreated to the greenhouses. This air may contain pollutants or mushroom spores SO that, at very least, HEPA filtration would be necessary. In current operation, synthetic nitrogen fertiliser is added to the growing medium. The EIP avoids this input; instead, the growing medium contains 15% dried solid digestate, following studies showing that substrates containing 10-20% solid digestate, 70-80% wheat straw, and 10-20% millet gave the highest yield for two species of mushrooms (Isikhuemhen et al., 2009a, 2009b).

Heating demand for the farms is based on BC's climate, with seasonal variations considered to estimate the natural gas required to supplement the biogas. Natural gas or upgraded biogas is burned in a modulating boiler; the higher heating values are assumed to be 38 MJ/m³ (Fortis BC, 2012) and 22 MJ/m³ (Bartok, 2004), respectively. Electricity is from the grid and is represented as the average mix for BC in 2007: 93.35% hydro, 5.1% natural gas, 1.45% biomass and 0.1% liquid fossil fuel (Environ- ment Canada, 2011). Transportation is modeled as heavy-duty diesel-- powered vehicles.
15: The parameters describing the activities making up the model EIP are summarised in Table 1. The EIP centres on a hypothetical dairy farm with 500 cows. This represents a medium-sized farm in North America but is larger than the average size in BC (see Section 4.2) and is much larger than a typical European farm (Hoang et al., 2020). For the pur- poses of the modelling exercise, greenhouse and mushroom production in the EIP are sized to fit with the dairy farm by eliminating natural gas demand in the summer season: biogas combustion supplies the summer heat demand of the three farms while the mushroom farm provides the additional CO2 for the greenhouses. In colder seasons, additional heat- ing is provided by natural gas.
In conventional practice, the manure and water from washing down

the dairy farm are stored in a covered tank before being spread onto nearby crop land using a vacuum tank tractor. In the EIP, the manure is treated in a digester, modeled as a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) controlled at 35 °C for mesophilic digestion. The raw biogas is assumed to contain 60% CH4, 37.9% CO2, 2% H2O, and 0.1% H2S, which is a typical composition with a few trace impurities neglected, primarily the mercaptans that are a main cause of odour (BC Ministry of Envi- ronment, 2010). The raw biogas is passed through two fixed-bed adsorption columns filled respectively with limestone and iron filings to remove water vapour and H2S. Fugitive emissions are assumed to be 0.5% of the raw biogas from the digester (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories et al., 2010) and 0.2% of the upgraded biogas from the adsorption system (Börjesson and Berglund, 2006).
An electric press-screw separator is modeled to separate the digestate

into a solid phase and a liquid phase. Solid digestate is assumed to be dried naturally with no energy consumption (Torenvliet, 2011). In winter when the climate is humid and cold in BC, additional drying is likely to be needed to prepare solid digestate for the use as animal bedding. Depending on the moisture content achievable by natural drying, the subsequent electrical drying may consume energy up to MJ/kg digestate (Rehl and Müller, 2011). However, compared to the rest of the EIP system, this energy consumption is insignificant and thus neglected. Part of the liquid digestate is spread on land using the tractor currently used for manure, and part of dried solid digestate is used in the dairy barn to substitute sawdust as animal bedding. The remaining digestate is exploited as value-added by-products to replace raw mate- rials used in greenhouse and mushroom farms (see below). Where sur- plus digestate needs to be composted (Scenario c; see Section 3.1), windrow composting is modeled.

The crop produced in the greenhouse in the EIP is modeled as pep- pers. Depending on the moisture content achievable by natural drying, the subsequent electrical drying may consume energy up to MJ/kg digestate (Rehl and Müller, 2011). However, compared to the rest of the EIP system, this energy consumption is insignificant and thus neglected. Part of the liquid digestate is spread on land using the tractor currently used for manure, and part of dried solid digestate is used in the dairy barn to substitute sawdust as animal bedding. The remaining digestate is exploited as value-added by-products to replace raw mate- rials used in greenhouse and mushroom farms (see below). Where sur- plus digestate needs to be composted (Scenario c; see Section 3.1), windrow composting is modeled.

The crop produced in the greenhouse in the EIP is modeled as pep- pers. Conventionally, the growing medium is a commercial rockwool product, and synthetic nitrogen fertiliser (urea or ammonium nitrate) is used as nitrogen fertilizer. In the EIP, dried solid digestate was taken to replace an equal mass of rockwool (Zhang et al., 2013) while liquid digestate substitutes synthetic nitrogen fertiliser at a rate that provides an equal nitrogen supply (Stoknes et al., 2016).

The mushroom farm is assumed to grow Pleurotus ostreatus, a crop for which it is feasible to substitute wheat straw in the growing medium by digestate (Isikhuemhen et al., 2009b). Mushrooms are typically grown in trays or on shelves on an indoor farm; the model is based on culti- vation with three levels of stacked trays. Ventilation rate varies throughout a mushroom growing cycle but, on average, 7.2 kg CO2 is released from 1 m² growing area per month to give an average con- centration of 815 ppm in mushroom houses (van Griensven, 1988). This is within the range 500-1,000 ppm typically used for stimulating plant growth in greenhouses (Luczynski and Portree, 2005). Therefore, the model assumes that ventilation air from the mushroom farm is piped untreated to the greenhouses. This air may contain pollutants or mushroom spores SO that, at very least, HEPA filtration would be necessary. In current operation, synthetic nitrogen fertiliser is added to the growing medium. The EIP avoids this input; instead, the growing medium contains 15% dried solid digestate, following studies showing that substrates containing 10-20% solid digestate, 70-80% wheat straw, and 10-20% millet gave the highest yield for two species of mushrooms (Isikhuemhen et al., 2009a, 2009b).

Heating demand for the farms is based on BC's climate, with seasonal variations considered to estimate the natural gas required to supplement the biogas. Natural gas or upgraded biogas is burned in a modulating boiler; the higher heating values are assumed to be 38 MJ/m³ (Fortis BC, 2012) and 22 MJ/m³ (Bartok, 2004), respectively. Electricity is from the grid and is represented as the average mix for BC in 2007: 93.35% hydro, 5.1% natural gas, 1.45% biomass and 0.1% liquid fossil fuel (Environ- ment Canada, 2011). Transportation is modeled as heavy-duty diesel-- powered vehicles.
20: The parameters describing the activities making up the model EIP are summarised in Table 1. The EIP centres on a hypothetical dairy farm with 500 cows. This represents a medium-sized farm in North America but is larger than the average size in BC (see Section 4.2) and is much larger than a typical European farm (Hoang et al., 2020). For the pur- poses of the modelling exercise, greenhouse and mushroom production in the EIP are sized to fit with the dairy farm by eliminating natural gas demand in the summer season: biogas combustion supplies the summer heat demand of the three farms while the mushroom farm provides the additional CO2 for the greenhouses. In colder seasons, additional heat- ing is provided by natural gas.
In conventional practice, the manure and water from washing down

the dairy farm are stored in a covered tank before being spread onto nearby crop land using a vacuum tank tractor. In the EIP, the manure is treated in a digester, modeled as a Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) controlled at 35 °C for mesophilic digestion. The raw biogas is assumed to contain 60% CH4, 37.9% CO2, 2% H2O, and 0.1% H2S, which is a typical composition with a few trace impurities neglected, primarily the mercaptans that are a main cause of odour (BC Ministry of Envi- ronment, 2010). The raw biogas is passed through two fixed-bed adsorption columns filled respectively with limestone and iron filings to remove water vapour and H2S. Fugitive emissions are assumed to be 0.5% of the raw biogas from the digester (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories et al., 2010) and 0.2% of the upgraded biogas from the adsorption system (Börjesson and Berglund, 2006).
An electric press-screw separator is modeled to separate the digestate

21: A base scenario (Scenario a) describes the independent conventional farms. Scenario b, called the "optimistic scenario", represents an EIP in which surplus digestate after meeting the internal demand is utilized to replace sawdust and synthetic nitrogen fertiliser in farms near the dairy farm. However, it is possible that local users of the surplus digestate cannot be found. Therefore, Scenario C describes the case in which surplus digestate is disposed as waste, representing the "moderate sce- nario": surplus solid digestate is composted, and surplus liquid digestate is sent to the municipal wastewater treatment system. Scenarios b and C produce the same output of agricultural products as Scenario a.

A comprehensive LCA was carried out to compare the environmental impacts of the three scenarios, following the approach set out in ISO 14044 (2006), to provide a complete analysis of the environmental impacts arising from the foreground agricultural activities and the in- puts to and outputs from the different systems. Inventory data were obtained from published databases, studies and reports, supplemented by primary data obtained from direct contacts; data sources are pre- sented in the Supplementary File, with further details available in Zhang (2013). Reliable data for BC or Canada were used where available, notably for the key activities of AD, energy supply and transportation, mainly from the GHGenius version 4.01 database developed by (S&T)2 Consultants Inc. (2012) for Natural Resources Canada. European data were adopted where Canadian data were not available, mainly from the Ecoinvent database developed by the Swiss Centre for Life Cycle In- ventories (Frischknecht et al., 2005).

Emission data were compiled, and the resulting impacts calculated using the SimaPro LCA software version 7.3.3. Table 2 shows the impact categories and characterization methods used; details of the methods are given in the SimaPro 7 Database Manual Methods Library (Goedkoop et al., 2010). Net non-renewable energy use and GHG emissions reflect the global impacts of the AD-centred EIP on resource depletion and climate change. For local impacts, aquatic acidification and eutrophi- cation are chosen to represent impacts on ecosystems, with respiratory effects and human toxicity to represent impacts on human health. In evaluating human toxicity, heavy metal emissions from handling of organic waste, including manure, straw, and digestate, are neglected as these substances are discharged to soil and water in all scenarios.

The LCA results, presented and discussed in Section 4.1, show re- ductions in each impact category achievable by implementing the EIP. To indicate the maximum possible reductions by linking independent farms in such EIPs throughout BC, these results were scaled to the total farming activity in BC. However, this is just an indicative estimate since the locations of the existing farms mean that they cannot all be linked into EIPs; this problem is explored further in Section 4.2. There were 126,800 dairy cows and heifers in BC in 2018 (BC Ministry of Agricul- ture, 2018) and 214,020 m² mushroom farms in BC in 2014 (British Columbia Agriculture in the Classroom Foundation, 2014). Based on the integration in this study, these farms can potentially support green- houses up to 685,000 m², representing more than 12% of the total 5.5 million m² of greenhouses in BC (British Columbia Agriculture in the Classroom Foundation, 2014). However, given the scale of the different activities in BC, the scope for the kind of EIP modeled here is limited by the scale of mushroom farming. Therefore, the maximum possible impact reduction potentials from implementing such EIPs in BC were calculated by scaling up by a factor of 66 (=214,020 from the optimistic scenario of this study.

To indicate the significance of such a change and identify the most significance impacts, the results were normalized by the current total impacts generated from all economic activities in BC. Total primary

energy consumption (Statistics Canada, 2014) and climate change im- pacts from BC's agriculture sector (BC Ministry of Environment, 2013) were obtained from government reports. In the absence of values spe- cific to BC, Canadian average values were used to estimate impacts from all other economic activities in BC. Total impacts of aquatic acidifica- tion, aquatic eutrophication, respiratory effects, and human toxicity from BC were calculated from the national average factors in impact/- person/year reported by Lautier et al. (2010) by multiplying by BC's human population - 4,683,139 as of July 2015 (BC Stats, 2016). Impacts of respiratory effects and human toxicity were further converted into an end-point impact indicator - Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) using IMPACT 2002+ damage factors.

Based on the integration in this study, these farms can potentially support green- houses up to 685,000 m², representing more than 12% of the total 5.5 million m² of greenhouses in BC (British Columbia Agriculture in the Classroom Foundation, 2014). However, given the scale of the different activities in BC, the scope for the kind of EIP modeled here is limited by the scale of mushroom farming. Therefore, the maximum possible impact reduction potentials from implementing such EIPs in BC were calculated by scaling up by a factor of 66 (=214,020 from the optimistic scenario of this study.

To indicate the significance of such a change and identify the most significance impacts, the results were normalized by the current total impacts generated from all economic activities in BC. Total primary

26: A base scenario (Scenario a) describes the independent conventional farms. Scenario b, called the "optimistic scenario", represents an EIP in which surplus digestate after meeting the internal demand is utilized to replace sawdust and synthetic nitrogen fertiliser in farms near the dairy farm. However, it is possible that local users of the surplus digestate cannot be found. Therefore, Scenario C describes the case in which surplus digestate is disposed as waste, representing the "moderate sce- nario": surplus solid digestate is composted, and surplus liquid digestate is sent to the municipal wastewater treatment system. Scenarios b and C produce the same output of agricultural products as Scenario a.

A comprehensive LCA was carried out to compare the environmental impacts of the three scenarios, following the approach set out in ISO 14044 (2006), to provide a complete analysis of the environmental impacts arising from the foreground agricultural activities and the in- puts to and outputs from the different systems. Inventory data were obtained from published databases, studies and reports, supplemented by primary data obtained from direct contacts; data sources are pre- sented in the Supplementary File, with further details available in Zhang (2013). Reliable data for BC or Canada were used where available, notably for the key activities of AD, energy supply and transportation, mainly from the GHGenius version 4.01 database developed by (S&T)2 Consultants Inc. (2012) for Natural Resources Canada. European data were adopted where Canadian data were not available, mainly from the Ecoinvent database developed by the Swiss Centre for Life Cycle In- ventories (Frischknecht et al., 2005).

Emission data were compiled, and the resulting impacts calculated using the SimaPro LCA software version 7.3.3. Table 2 shows the impact categories and characterization methods used; details of the methods are given in the SimaPro 7 Database Manual Methods Library (Goedkoop et al., 2010). Net non-renewable energy use and GHG emissions reflect the global impacts of the AD-centred EIP on resource depletion and climate change. For local impacts, aquatic acidification and eutrophi- cation are chosen to represent impacts on ecosystems, with respiratory effects and human toxicity to represent impacts on human health. In evaluating human toxicity, heavy metal emissions from handling of organic waste, including manure, straw, and digestate, are neglected as these substances are discharged to soil and water in all scenarios.

The LCA results, presented and discussed in Section 4.1, show re- ductions in each impact category achievable by implementing the EIP. To indicate the maximum possible reductions by linking independent farms in such EIPs throughout BC, these results were scaled to the total farming activity in BC. However, this is just an indicative estimate since the locations of the existing farms mean that they cannot all be linked into EIPs; this problem is explored further in Section 4.2. There were 126,800 dairy cows and heifers in BC in 2018 (BC Ministry of Agricul- ture, 2018) and 214,020 m² mushroom farms in BC in 2014 (British Columbia Agriculture in the Classroom Foundation, 2014). Based on the integration in this study, these farms can potentially support green- houses up to 685,000 m², representing more than 12% of the total 5.5 million m² of greenhouses in BC (British Columbia Agriculture in the Classroom Foundation, 2014). However, given the scale of the different activities in BC, the scope for the kind of EIP modeled here is limited by the scale of mushroom farming. Therefore, the maximum possible impact reduction potentials from implementing such EIPs in BC were calculated by scaling up by a factor of 66 (=214,020 from the optimistic scenario of this study.

To indicate the significance of such a change and identify the most significance impacts, the results were normalized by the current total impacts generated from all economic activities in BC. Total primary

27: The role of EIPs in managing the principal agricultural nutrient el- ements - nitrogen and phosphorus - was explored by comparing the regional demand for N and P in fertilisers with the regional flows in organic waste. The difference indicates the seriousness of the nutrient management problem: a positive balance indicates a surplus of nutrient from organic waste that must be exported to prevent nutrient runoff. N and P balances were formulated for the Lower Mainland (comprising Fraser Valley and Metro Vancouver) and, for comparison, for three other regions of BC with intensive cattle farming: Thompson-Okanagan, Car- iboo, and Peace River, to the East and North of the Lower Mainland.

Regional nutrient demand was estimated from the acreage of different crops planted in each region (British Columbia, 2017), the ratio of crop residues left in the field as organic fertilizers (Statistics Canada, 2019c), and the recommended fertilization rates (Alberta Agriculture Food and Rural Development, 2004). The demand for organic nitrogen is

then estimated from the ratio of manure nitrogen (N) to total N fertil- ization. This ratio is generally less than 30% throughout Canada (Huffman et al., 2011), in part because undigested manure releases N slowly and so must be used in combination with quick-release synthetic fertiliser. However, the value of 30% results from other factors, such as low cost of fertilisers and concern for yield rather than sustainability, and is not necessarily optimal. In this study, it is assumed that manure can supply 50% of the total N fertilization in Southern BC, representing an optimistic value favorable to the use of undigested organic waste as fertilizer.

Regional nutrient supply was estimated for the main organic waste streams, i.e., animal and food waste. Nutrient supply from animal waste was obtained based on the nutrient content of cattle manure (Nennich et al., 2005) and poultry manure (Nicholson et al., 1996), the average manure generation per animal (Hofmann and Beaulieu, 2001), and the herd size (British Columbia, 2017). Nutrient supply from food waste was obtained based on regional population, average food waste generation per capita (BC Stats, 2010), and nutrient content (Zhang et al., 2007).

29: In this section, the environmental impacts for the base scenario (a), optimistic EIP scenario (b), and moderate EIP scenario (c) across the seasons are presented and compared. The numerical values are given in the Supplementary File. Activities that make significant contributions for each impact category are specified in the figure legends. Negative values represent savings resulting from substitution of inputs by trans- fers within the EIP.
Fig. 2. Contributions of different activities in the three scenarios to (a) climate-forcing emissions and (b) non-renewable energy consumption.
30: Climate change impacts are calculated by aggregating the GHG emissions according to their 100-year GWP values. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), more than 90% of the GHG emissions in the base scenario come from the upstream processes, combustion of natural gas and manure storage. Replacing natural gas, for heating by biogas and for CO2 enrichment by flue gases from biogas combustion and vent gases from the mushroom farm, saves more than 20 TJ per year of non-renewable energy in the optimistic scenario, corresponding to 58% of consumption in the base case (Fig. 2(b)). This is the main cause of the reduction in climate change shown in Fig. 2(a). Manure handling, especially spreading, is the main source of CH4 and N2O emissions in the base scenario; anaerobic digestion in the EIP reduces these emissions. Substitution of sawdust and nitrogen fertilizers with surplus digestate leads to further slight re- ductions in GHG emissions, primarily N2O emissions from production of synthetic nitrogen fertilisers (Wood and Cowie, 2004). In total, climate change impact in the optimistic scenario is reduced by more than 2 kilotonne CO2-eq/year, which corresponds to 69% of the impact for the base case.

Fossil CO2 emissions in the EIP arise mostly from fuel and electricity consumption for operating the digester system and digestate applica- tion. The upstream processes for producing the energy contribute to fugitive emissions of fossil CH4. Biogenic CH4 is emitted mainly from digestion and biogas upgrading. However, these emissions are small compared with the GHG reductions explained above.

32: Aquatic acidification and eutrophication represent hotspots for local environmental impacts caused by conventional agricultural activities. These impacts are shown in Fig. 3 for the base scenario (a), optimistic
33: In the base scenario (a), aquatic acidification results almost solely from manure treatment, which releases large amounts of H2S and NH3; H2S is mainly generated under anaerobic conditions (Atia et al., 1999) so that emissions from manure storage are higher than from land spreading. Oxides of sulphur and nitrogen also contribute slightly to aquatic acidification. Most of the SOx emissions arise from upstream processing of natural gas, with fuel production and natural gas sweet- ening as the main contributors ((S&T)2 Consultants Inc., 2012). Other emissions arise from use of fossil fuels in production of rockwool and straw. Emissions of NOx arise mainly from upstream processing and combustion of natural gas, and from fuels used directly or indirectly in manure spreading and straw production. The major relevant pollutants for aquatic eutrophication are NH3 and NOx, but the results parallel those for acidification.

In the optimistic EIP scenario (b), aquatic acidification is reduced by 12 tonne SO2-eq/year (i.e. 52% of the impacts in the base case) and eutrophication is reduced by 330 kg PO4-eq/year (13% of the base case). There are no significant seasonal variations because natural gas and biogas are not important contributors to these impacts. Manure treat- ment is completely avoided, resulting in substantial reduction in emis- sions of H2S, SOx, NOx, and NH3. Small amounts of H2S are emitted in the leaked biogas, which contains H2S as one of its main impurities. Digestate application is the most significant source of NH3 emission, due to high ammonium concentration in the digestate. Biogas production, surplus digestate disposal, and digestate application are the principal sources of SOx and NOx emissions. Overall, in the optimistic scenario, the aquatic acidification and eutrophication impacts resulting from the above-mentioned emissions are still lower than those in the base sce- nario. In the moderate scenario, emission reductions are slightly lower
Fig. 3. Contributions of different activities in the three scenarios to (a) acidification and (b) eutrophication.
than those in the optimistic scenario, due to additional environmental impacts of the disposal of surplus digestate.

36: The human health effects considered are toxicity and impacts on the human respiratory system. In the base scenario, manure storage and spreading are the main sources of the inorganic emissions that cause respiratory effects: NH3, H2S, NOx, SOx, and Particulate Matter (PM); see Fig. 4(a). PM in particular is a common and key pollutant causing respiratory effects; in the base scenario, PM also comes from straw production, rockwool production, and upstream processing and com- bustion of natural gas. Note that even though straw is a byproduct of crop farming, its use is also associated with a considerable degree of health impacts, for two main reasons: (1) the liquid fuel used to collect and transport straw, and (2) the additional use of synthetic fertilisers to compensate for the nutrient contents in straw removed from the field.

In the EIP, emissions of inorganic pollutants from liquid digestate application are significant yet still lower than from manure spreading. Biogas combustion has a higher impact due to higher emission factors of NOx and PM. Biogas upgrading also generates indirect PM emissions from processing the limestone and iron milling adsorbents. Overall, the reduction in the optimistic scenario of the EIP over the base scenario is 156 kg PM2.5-eq/year which is 14% of the emissions in the base case.

Respiratory effects from organic emissions are related primarily to NMVOCs, pentane, butane, and benzene (Frischknecht et al., 2005). As shown in Fig. 4(b), the main source of NMVOC emissions in the base scenario is natural gas upstream processing and combustion. Rockwool and straw production also emit NMVOCs. In the EIP, the main source of NMVOC is the continual use of natural gas in colder seasons, whereas the contribution from digestion and digestate application is nugatory. In the moderate scenario, the disposal of surplus digestate generates additional NMVOC emissions. In the optimistic scenario, the impact reduction is up
Fig. 4. Contributions of different activities in the three scenarios to (a) respiratory inorganics, (b) respiratory organics, and (c) human toxicity impacts.
to 46 kg C2H4-eq/year, which represents 57% of the impacts in the base case.

Human toxicity impact represents the combined effect from toxic air pollutants and heavy metals discharged to the environmental. In the base scenario, human toxicity impacts result from manure management, and production and use of natural gas, rock wool, and straw. Together with the impacts from manure treatment, biogas combustion, and rockwool production, the total impact is up to 317 kg C2H3Cl-eq/month. The impact from straw production, manure treatment, and rockwool production can be avoided in the EIP and a further reduction results from reducing demand for natural gas. Overall, the optimistic scenario of the EIP achieves a reduction of 3.3 tonne C2H3Cl-eq/year, i.e. 88% of the impacts in the base case.

40: Table 3 shows the impacts for the optimistic scenario, normalized against the total impacts from economic activities within BC. The most significant of the improvements in the EIP is reduced non-renewable energy consumption. Reductions in climate change, acidification, eutrophication, and human toxicity are moderate, around 0.1% of the provincial totals, while the reductions in respiratory effects are nuga- tory. However, it is worth noting that impacts associated with material and energy production outside the province are included in the LCA but not in the reference figures used for normalization; examples are straw, fertilizer, and fuels including diesel and natural gas. This illustrates a common problem when normalising LCA results derived on a con- sumption basis against macroeconomic figures for production in a spe- cific area. The way to resolve this inconsistency needs to be investigated once more detailed information like the supply chains of the commod- ities become available.
41: As noted in Section 2.1, intensive livestock husbandry, greenhouse production and mushroom farms in BC are concentrated in the Lower Mainland area in the South of the Province. To explore the feasibility of implementing EIP systems, the proximity of suitable dairy farms, vege- table greenhouses, and mushroom farms has been surveyed. As shown in Fig. 5, a large number of farms are located in clusters (BC Holstein News, 2017) within a radius of around 10 km in the Lower Mainland to the South of the Fraser River (Google search, 2017), confirming that the approach is practicable even with the existing farms. In addition to the scenarios analyzed here which uses AD units located at the dairy farm, it would be feasible to transport dairy manure to other farms and to pipe CO2 between mushroom farms and greenhouses.

Although the type of EIP proposed here seems to be feasible, the details need to be adjusted for the existing agricultural operations in BC. A farm with 500 cows, as envisaged in the model, is a typical large dairy farm in North America but larger than the BC average which is 110 cows (British Columbia, 2017). About 50% of BC's vegetable greenhouses are between 8,090 m² and 40,500 m² (British Columbia, 2017), compared to 10,400 m2 used in this study. The average size of mushroom farms is about 30,500 m² (BC Ministry of Agriculture, 2017), which is much larger than the modeled size of 3,250 m² implying that there is more CO2

available from a typical existing mushroom farm. However, the EIP concept is flexible. For example, a mushroom farm could be linked to several greenhouse and dairy farms. Alternatively, the anaerobic digester could be installed on a smaller dairy farm, such as the average in BC, and combined with more ventilated CO2 from a larger mushroom farm, with the size of the greenhouse selected based on the CO2 and energy balances. In such cases, benefits from integration are sub-optimal but still represent improvements over conventional practice without integration; the analysis shows that integrated use of CO2 is the most important feature.

44: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

Netherlands, the livestock density is 2.7 cattle per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.38 tonne per hectare per year. In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

46: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

Netherlands, the livestock density is 2.7 cattle per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.38 tonne per hectare per year. In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

48: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

50: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

51: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

Netherlands, the livestock density is 2.7 cattle per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.38 tonne per hectare per year. In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

53: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

Netherlands, the livestock density is 2.7 cattle per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.38 tonne per hectare per year. In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

55: Anaerobic digestion (AD) can potentially improve nutrient usage efficiency of animal manure, because AD converts nutrient elements from organic to inorganic forms, which are more readily available to plants (Möller and Müller, 2012). However, AD also concentrates nu- trients locally. In a case study in the Netherlands, Hoang et al. (2020) concluded that mono-digestion of cattle manure does not alleviate the nitrogen issues in manure management. A simple nutrient flow analysis was therefore undertaken to explore the implications for regional nutrient management of integrating different farms.

The results of the nutrient flow analysis are shown in Fig. 6 for different regions in BC. The abscissa shows different BC regions inves- tigated. The ordinate shows the balances, for nitrogen and phosphorus, between the input in organic waste (positive) and the demand on waste- sourced nutrients for agricultural activities (negative); positive balances indicate the extent of nutrient surplus while negative values indicate demand for synthetic inputs. The combination of intensive livestock farming and shortage of agricultural land generates a serious nitrogen surplus from organic waste in areas such as the Lower Mainland. This surplus can lead to higher environmental impacts, as are already observed in other areas with intensive livestock production, such as southern Sweden (Cederberg, 2002) and Brittany, France (Cederberg, 2002; Chrisafis, 2019). On the other hand, the other regions of BC with intensive livestock farming have abundant agricultural land, mostly pasture, and thus much higher land/animal ratio and demands for fertilization (British Columbia, 2017). Therefore, it has been suggested that, to alleviate local environmental impacts associated with nutrient surplus, manure could be exported from the Lower Mainland to adjacent regions (Timmenga and Associates Inc., 2003). However, extended transportation would significantly increase costs, along with other environmental impacts, and increase the likelihood of improper disposal of animal manure (see Section 2.1).

Using the digestate in the EIP to substitute synthetic fertilizer offers an alternative to exporting surplus manure. As shown in Table 4, in the specific case of the Lower Mainland of BC, implementation of AD can potentially increase the utilisation rate of nitrogen in organic wastes from 18% to 36%, eliminate demand for synthetic nitrogen fertilizer, and thus reduce the nitrogen surplus from 35% to 19%. Furthermore, integration of AD with greenhouse and mushroom farms can increase the efficiency of nitrogen use by a further 3%.

The difference between this conclusion and the conclusion of Hoang et al. (2020), that AD does not improve local nutrient management, can be explained by the differences between the Netherlands and southern BC in livestock density and utilisation of manure for fertilisation. In the

Netherlands, the livestock density is 2.7 cattle per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.38 tonne per hectare per year. In comparison, the livestock density in southern BC is 1.7 cattle and 226 poultry per hectare, equivalent to a manure-sourced nitrogen load of 0.44 tonne per hectare per year, 17% higher than in the Netherlands. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.

The analysis also confirms that imple- menting such EIPs should be technically feasible, given the geographical distribution of the agricultural activities. Once more local peer-reviewed data become available, the analysis could be further improved and the model could be verified. In terms of manure use, the ratio of manure N to total N fertilization in the Netherlands exceeds 60% (Hoang et al., 2020); by contrast, this ratio across Canada is less than 30% (Huffman et al., 2011), indicating much poorer utilisation of manure. In a region with such high livestock density and poor nutrient management as Southern BC, AD integrated into an EIP can profoundly change the nitrogen balance, increasing the uti- lisation of manure-sourced nitrogen.
